behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is discovering and sorting actual migration files from the filesystem and verifying they're applied to a real database in the correct order. A pure mock filesystem could miss bugs in file discovery logic, and a mocked database wouldn't verify actual application order. The ordering logic is deterministic, but integrating filesystem discovery with database execution requires real infrastructure to catch off-by-one errors, sorting bugs, or path handling issues.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test database with real migration files named sequentially (001_init.sql, 002_add_users.sql, 003_add_posts.sql). Run the migration runner once, then verify the database state reflects the correct application order (e.g., tables exist in dependency order, foreign key constraints reference tables created earlier). Query the _migrations table to confirm migrations are recorded in ascending order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is verifying that the migration runner correctly queries persistent database state in the _migrations table across separate runs and skips already-applied migrations. Mocking the database would hide issues with state persistence, table querying, or record comparison. Requires a real database with data persisting between test runs to catch bugs where duplicates are inserted or migrations are re-executed.
behaviors.test_description: Apply a set of migrations to a real test database, verify the _migrations table contains one record per applied migration. In the same test session (or separate invocation), run the migration runner again against the same database. Verify that no migrations are re-executed by checking: (1) database schema is unchanged from the first run, (2) _migrations table still has exactly one record per migration (no duplicates), (3) migration files are discovered but skipped.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is verifying that PostgreSQL transaction rollback semantics actually work as expected when migration SQL fails. Mocking the database would hide real rollback behavior, partial-apply bugs, or constraint violations that only surface with actual SQL execution and transaction control. Cannot reliably simulate PostgreSQL's ROLLBACK behavior in a mock—requires real database with real transaction semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test database with multiple migration files. Make one migration intentionally fail (e.g., CREATE TABLE with invalid syntax, or INSERT violating a constraint). Run the migration runner and verify: (1) the failed migration's changes are rolled back (prior schema state is restored), (2) the failed migration is not recorded in _migrations, (3) migrations that came after the failed migration in the batch are not applied, (4) no partial state or data corruption exists.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is actual concurrent execution with real OS process scheduling and database-level locking semantics. Mocking concurrency or database locks would hide race conditions, deadlocks, duplicate-apply bugs, or lost-update anomalies. Single-process threading cannot faithfully reproduce the OS scheduling and distributed lock contention that real processes face. Requires real separate Node.js processes running concurrently against a real database.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two separate Node.js processes, each running the migration runner against the same test database. Synchronize them to collide (e.g., use a shared file or coordinated start timing to ensure both processes read migration files and attempt application in parallel). Verify: (1) both processes complete without deadlock or constraint errors, (2) _migrations table contains each migration exactly once (no duplicate rows), (3) database schema is correct and complete, (4) no partial or corrupted states exist.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is verifying that the migration runner correctly creates the _migrations metadata table during initialization on a fresh database before executing any migration SQL. Mocking table creation would hide syntax errors, schema definition bugs, or initialization-ordering bugs. Requires a real database in a fresh/empty state to catch edge cases like table already exists, permission errors, or incorrect column definitions.
behaviors.test_description: Use a fresh test database (or cleaned to empty state), verify the _migrations table does not exist before the test. Run the migration runner with at least one migration file present. Verify: (1) the _migrations table is created before any migrations are applied, (2) migration records are inserted into this table, (3) the table schema contains expected columns (e.g., migration_name, applied_at timestamps), (4) subsequent runs do not attempt to recreate the table.
